rulings.Case Title: CARYL HARRISON VS. DEREK A. TAGGARD MD et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Answers And Production Of Documents At Deposition; Request For Order Awarding Monetary Sanctions Against Defts And His Attorney
rulings.Rulings: On calendar for Monday, June 16, 2014, Line 1, PLAINTIFF CARYL HARRISON's Motion To Compel Answers And Production Of Documents At Deposition; Request For Order Awarding Monetary Sanctions Against Defendants And His Attorney. Pro Tem Judge Peter Van Zandt, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff Caryl Harrison's Motion to Compel Answers at Deposition is DENIED. It is undisputed that the Defendant Derek A. Taggard, M.D. is not a disclosed expert in the case. During the comprehensive and skillfully conducted deposition, Dr. Taggard was asked to interpret a CT scan taken on October 17, 2011 for the subtle, but obvious purpose of extracting an impermissible after-the-fact impression. (County of Los Angeles (Martinez) v. Superior Court (1990) 224 Cal.App.3d 1446, 1455-1456) Based upon a review of the deposition excerpts provided in the moving and opposition papers, Mr. Bostwick was given wide latitude in obtaining Dr. Taggard's impressions and reasons for his action or lack of action at the time the medical procedure was performed and was only halted when questions sought Dr. Taggard's present interpretation of the CT scan. Any party who contests this tentative ruling must send an email to peter.vanzandt@leclairryan.com or a fax to 415.391.8766 with a copy to all other parties by 4pm one day prior to the hearing stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)
rulings.Judge: Judge Pro Tem: Peter Van Zandt

rulings.Case Title: JEREMY AKSDAL-JANSEN VS. JENNY LEE et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants Motion To Have Admission Requets Deemed Admitted
rulings.Rulings: On calendar for Monday, June 16, 2014, Line 3, DEFENDANT JENNY LEE's Motion To Have Admission Requests Deemed Admitted. Pro Tem Judge Peter Van Zandt, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Defendant JENNY LEE's Motion to Have Admission Requests Deemed Admitted is GRANTED. Plaintiff JEREMY AKSDAL-JANSEN has failed to serve any response to Requests for Admission served on February 26, 2014. Counsel for defendant JENNY LEE sent a letter to plaintiff JEREMY AKSDAL-JANSEN on April 23, 2014 giving him 10 days to respond which he has failed to do. The letter erroneously states that the Requests for Admission were served on March 3, 2014, but the court finds no prejudice arising from this error. Pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 2033.280 (a)-(b) the Requests for Admission are deemed admitted. Sanctions in the amount of $60 are to be paid by plaintiff JEREMY AKSDAL-JANSEN to Stratman, Patterson & Hunter within 20 days of the service of Notice of Entry of the Order arising from this hearing. Any party who contests this tentative ruling must send an email to peter.vanzandt@leclairryan.com or a fax to 415.391.8766 with a copy to all other parties by 4pm one day prior to the hearing stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)
rulings.Judge: Judge Pro Tem: Peter Van Zandt

rulings.Case Title: GORDON DRYSDALE VS. BACCHUS MANAGEMENT GROUP, LLC, A CALIFORNIA et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Cross-Defendant Gordon Drysdale'S Notice Of Special Motion To Strike First Amended Cross-Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, June 16, 2014, Line 9, PLAINTIFF GORDON DRYSDALE Cross-Defendant Gordon Drysdale'S Notice Of Special Motion To Strike First Amended Cross-Complaint The court grants the anti-SLAPP motion to strike. The gravamen of Bacchus' breach of contract cause of action is that Mr. Drysdale breached the parties' written LLC agreements by alleging that an oral agreement governs the parties' relationship and/or asserting liabilities inconsistent with the written LLC agreements. The cause of action implicates petitioning activity and Mr. Drysdale meets the first prong of CCP 425.16. See Wentland v. Wass (2005) 126 Cal.App.4th 1484. Bacchus is therefore obligated to show a probability of prevailing. First, Bacchus fails to make that showing because it presents no competent evidence regarding damages. Second, the litigation privilege bars the cause of action. Article 2.06 of the LLC agreements does not bar lawsuits, it merely limits relief in certain circumstances. Applying the privilege does not frustrate the purpose of the LLC agreements. In addition, as in Pollock v. Superior Court, (1991) 229 Cal.App.3d 26, Bacchus has another remedy to vindicate its rights. All parties agree that Bacchus can raise the written LLC agreements as a defense to Mr. Drysdale's action. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/eg

rulings.Case Title: FIRENZA PINI VS. NESTLE PURINA PETCARE COMPANY et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Re-Notice Of Mtn And Mtn To Transfer Venue
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, June 16, 2014, Line 10, DEFENDANT NESTLE PURINA PETCARE COMPANY's Re-Notice Of Mtn And Mtn To Transfer Venue Granted. This matter is transferred to Kern County. "In cases with mixed causes of action, a motion for change of venue must be granted on the entire complaint if the defendant is entitled to a change of venue on any one cause of action." (Brown v. Superior Court (1984) 37 Cal.3d 477, 488.) Plaintiff does not dispute that venue is not proper in San Francisco for Defendant Nestle Purina Petcare Co. ("Defendant"), but argues that Defendant's principal place of business is not located in Kern County. "A corporation is bound for venue purposes by its designation of its 'principal place of business' in corporate documents filed with the Secretary of State." (Weil & Brown, Cal. Practice Guide: Civ. Proc. Before Trial (Rutter Group 2013), par. 3:521.1.) This rule applies to foreign corporations. (Gutierrez v. Sup. Ct. (1966) 243 Cal.App.2d 710, 728.) Defendant designates its principal place of business in California as located in Kern County on its Statement of Information filed with the Secretary of State. (Declaration of Stacy Bacilek, Ex. A.) Where several venues would be proper, the court must transfer to the venue selected by the moving defendant. (Cubic Corp. v. Sup. Ct.(1986) 186 Cal.App.3d 622, 625.) Because Kern County is a proper venue and Defendant seeks to have the matter transferred there, this matter must be transferred to Kern County. Plaintiff shall pay Defendant reasonable fees incurred in filing this motion in the amount of $ 4,575.00 pursuant to CCP sec. 396b. Plaintiff offers no evidence in support of her arguments as to why she did not respond to Defendant's requests to stipulate to transferring venue. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/eg

rulings.Case Title: BIRITE RESTAURANT SUPPLY INC., A CORPORATION DBA VS. JPMD LLC, A CALIFORNIA LIABILITY COMPANY DBA et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, June 16, 2014, Line 11, PLAINTIFF BIRITE RESTAURANT SUPPLY INC., A CORPORATION's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Denied. CCP sec. 437c(a) provides that a motion for summary judgment may be made "at any time after 60 days have elapsed since the general appearance in the action or proceeding of each party against whom the motion is directed or at any earlier time after the general appearance that the court, with or without notice and upon good cause shown, may direct." Defendant made its general appearance when it filed a general denial on March 4, 2014. Plaintiff filed this motion on March 17, 2014, less than the 60 days required by statute. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and in compliance with Code of Civil Procedure 437c(g) and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/eg

rulings.Case Title: DINO TURANO VS. PASTA POMODORO, INC. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, June 16, 2014, Line 14, DEFENDANT PASTA POMODORO, INCORPORATED'S MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT Granted with 20 days leave to amend. Punitive damages are available "[i]n an action for the breach of an obligation not arising from contract." (CCP sec. 3294(a).) As pled, the fraud in the inducement cause of action arises from the termination of Plaintiff's employment contract. (See Hine v. Dittrich (1991) 228 Cal.App.3d 59, 65, "[a]s long as the alleged injury would not have occurred but for the employment termination¿the employee is generally limited to a contractual remedy.") Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/EG

rulings.Case Title: TYLER GERSTEIN VS. EXPRESS MESSENGER SYSTEMS, INC. DBA ONTRAC et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn And Mtn To Intervene And To Stay And Abate This Action Due To Another Action Pending
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, June 16, 2014, Line 15, INTERVENOR BERGLUND, LISA, PERSONAL REPRESENTATIVE OF THE Ntc Of Mtn And Mtn To Intervene And To Stay And Abate This Action Due To Another Action Pending The court denies Ms. Berglund's Motion to Intervene and Stay. First, the court determines that Ms. Berglund's interests, i.e., the interests of Tyler Gerstein, are adequately represented in this action and intervention is unnecessary. See CCP 387(b). Second, even if the court were to permit intervention, the court finds that this action should not be stayed under the doctrine of concurrent exclusive jurisdiction given the particular circumstances. The doctrine "should not be a rigid inflexible principle, particularly where as here, the multiple lawsuits were brought on behalf of minors, filed essentially simultaneously, and served within a brief period. Under these circumstances, there is no principled basis for requiring a court to favor one lawsuit over the other solely because of the timing of the lawsuits." Williams v. Sup. Crt. (2007) 147 Cal.App.4th 36, 53. In this case, the San Mateo and San Francisco actions were filed and served almost simultaneously. The court determines that allowing the San Francisco wrongful death action to move forward is better as that action is being prosecuted by Tyler Gerstein's custodial father. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/eg
